concepts.definition: A material component is not present in the material entity
A material component comprises the entire material entity by volume, excluding negligible impurities
A material component comprises more than 50% of the material entity by volume
A material component comprises between 5% and 50% of the material entity by volume
A material component is confirmed to exist in the material entity but proportion has not been quantified
A material component comprises between 0.1% and 5% of the material entity by volume
A material component comprises less than 0.1% of the material entity by volume
The proportion of a material component varies significantly within the material entity or across specimens
A name by which a geologic material is frequently referred, without reference to any formal usage or structure nor sanctioned by an authoritative body
The name of a geological material for cataloging or presentation purposes.
A name derived from an authoritative classification system at any rank
A name assigned by an authoritative body based on a defined set of unambiguous criteria and belonging to a formal nomenclature
The name of a mineralogical group a specimen belongs to. Groups are defined as a set of mineral species with essentially the same crystal structure and composed of chemically similar elements.
An alias of a mineral name used within a historical context or derived from a historical source
A mineral name that has received formal approval by the International Mineralogical Association (IMA)

A name defined in prior standards or classification systems but has since been removed from those authoritative sources. Includes names designated by the International Mineralogical Association as discredited or obsolete.
Name of the solid solution series to which a specimen belongs.
A group name based on a shared crystal structure and composition of chemically similar elements
An alias of a mineral species no longer recognized as the official classification
The name of a mineralogical specimen that is pending approval as an offical name by the International Mineralogical Association (IMA)
A mineral that is a type of the mineral species, distinguished by color or other optical phenomenon or characteristic
A geologic material that is extraterrestrial in origin or derived from a geological process involving an extraterrestrial material (impactite)
Any remains, trace, or imprint of a plant or animal preserved in the Earth's crust since some past geologic or prehistoric time; loosely, any evidence of past life.
Any mineral or naturally occurring substance that, when cut and polished, has sufficient beauty, rarity, and durability for use as a personal adornment or other ornament.
A material entity comprised of a natural amount of matter generated by some geologic process, includes synthetic material intended to represent a geological material

A natural, solid object, derived from a celestial body, that was transported by natural means from the body on which it formed to a region outside the dominant gravitational influence of that body and that later collided with a natural or artificial body larger than itself.
A naturally occurring element or chemical compound of homogeneous composition, normally with a crystalline structure, and resulting from some geological process on Earth or in extraterrestrial bodies
A naturally occurring geological material from which economically valuable minerals can be extracted.

Consolidated aggregate of one or more EarthMaterials, or a body of undifferentiated mineral matter, or of solid organic material. Includes mineral aggregates such as granite, shale, marble; glassy matter such as obsidian; and organic material such a coal. Excludes unconsolidated materials.
A geological material, commonly cylindrical in shape, recovered from a borehole through drilling
Astromaterial returned from sample collection missions to celestial objects (e.g. comets, asteroids, the Moon, Mars)
Man-made materials created in a laboratory that are intended to representative of natural occurring geological material
A sedimentary structure produced by the life activities (other than growth) of some organism
A geologic material composed of an aggregation of particles that do not adhere to each other strongly enough that the aggregate can be considered a solid in its own right.

A sample or specimen containing acicular or fibrous asbestos (serpentine or amphibole) minerals, either naturally or as part of storage materials, which can cause internal damage if breathed or ingested.
A role played by a chemical compound which is known to induce a process of carcinogenesis by corrupting normal cellular pathways, leading to the acquistion of tumoral capabilities.
A material that causes visible destruction of, or irreversible alterations in living tissue by chemical action at the site of contact.
A solid or liquid chemical which is in itself capable by chemical reaction of producing gas at such a temperature and pressure and at such a speed as to cause damage to the surroundings.
A sample or preparation containing inflammable, flammable or flame-sustaining materials, whether naturally, or as part of treatment or storage materials.
Any material containing unstable atoms that emit ionizing radiation as it decays.
A chemical or biological substance which is capable of causing illness, debilitation or death, to living organisms, tissues or cells.
